---
title: "Getting Started with Epinowcast: Nowcasting"
description: "A quick start example demonstrating use of epinowcast to nowcast hospital admissions."
author: Epinowcast Team
opengraph:
  image:
    src: figures/getting-started-nowcasting-comparison-1.png
output:
  bookdown::html_vignette2:
    fig_caption: yes
    code_folding: show
pkgdown:
  as_is: true
bibliography: library.bib
csl: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/citation-style-language/styles/master/apa-numeric-superscript-brackets.csl
link-citations: true
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Getting Started with Epinowcast: Nowcasting}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

# Quick start

In this quick start, we use `epinowcast` to nowcast COVID-19 hospitalisations in Germany.
We start with the default model, identify where it falls short, and then build two improved models with different delay assumptions.
This demonstrates how `epinowcast` serves as a modelling toolkit rather than a single fixed model.
Examples using more complex models are available in other package vignettes and in the papers referenced in the literature vignette.

# Package

In this quick start, we also use the `data.table` and `ggplot2` packages.
These are both installed as dependencies when `epinowcast` is installed.
Note that all output from `epinowcast` is readily useable with other tools, including `tidyverse` packages (see [here](https://mgimond.github.io/rug_2019_12/Index.html) for a comparison).


``` r
library(epinowcast)
library(data.table)
library(ggplot2)
```

# Data

Nowcasting of right-truncated case counts involves the estimation of reporting delays for recently reported data.
For this, we need case counts both by when they were diagnosed (e.g. when someone tests positive; often called "reference date") and by when they were reported (i.e. when administratively recorded via public health surveillance; often called "report date").
The difference between the reference date and the report date is the reporting delay.
For this quick start, we use data from the [Robert Koch Institute via the Germany Nowcasting hub](https://github.com/KITmetricslab/hospitalization-nowcast-hub/wiki/Truth-data#role-an-definition-of-the-seven-day-hospitalization-incidence).
These data represent hospitalisation counts by date of positive test and date of test report in Germany up to October 1, 2021.

## Filtering

We first filter to create a snapshot of retrospective data available 43 days before October 1, 2021 that contains 43 days of data.
Then, we create the nowcast target which is the latest available hospitalisations by date of positive test.
This allows us to visualise how a nowcast made at the time compares to what was ultimately reported.


``` r
nat_germany_hosp <-
  germany_covid19_hosp[location == "DE"][age_group == "00+"] |>
  enw_filter_report_dates(latest_date = "2021-10-01")

retro_nat_germany <- nat_germany_hosp |>
  enw_filter_report_dates(remove_days = 43) |>
  enw_filter_reference_dates(include_days = 43)
retro_nat_germany
#>      reference_date location age_group confirm report_date
#>              <IDat>   <fctr>    <fctr>   <int>      <IDat>
#>   1:     2021-07-08       DE       00+      25  2021-07-08
#>   2:     2021-07-09       DE       00+      10  2021-07-09
#>   3:     2021-07-10       DE       00+      23  2021-07-10
#>   4:     2021-07-11       DE       00+      14  2021-07-11
#>   5:     2021-07-12       DE       00+       3  2021-07-12
#>  ---                                                      
#> 942:     2021-07-09       DE       00+      46  2021-08-18
#> 943:     2021-07-10       DE       00+      64  2021-08-19
#> 944:     2021-07-08       DE       00+      53  2021-08-18
#> 945:     2021-07-09       DE       00+      46  2021-08-19
#> 946:     2021-07-08       DE       00+      53  2021-08-19
```

This data is already in a format that can be used with `epinowcast`, as it contains

- a reference date (column `reference_date`): the date of the observation, in this example the date of a positive test
- a report date (column `report_date`): the date of report for a given set of observations by reference date
- a count (column `confirm`): the total (i.e. cumulative) number of hospitalisations by reference date and report date.

The package also provides a range of tools to convert data from line list, incidence, or other common formats into the required format (see [Data converters](https://package.epinowcast.org/dev/reference/index.html#data-converters)).


``` r
latest_germany_hosp <- nat_germany_hosp |>
  enw_obs_at_delay(max_delay = 43) |>
  enw_filter_reference_dates(
    remove_days = 43, include_days = 43
  )
head(latest_germany_hosp, n = 10)
#>     reference_date .group location age_group confirm report_date
#>             <IDat>  <num>   <fctr>    <fctr>   <int>      <IDat>
#>  1:     2021-07-08      1       DE       00+      53  2021-08-19
#>  2:     2021-07-09      1       DE       00+      46  2021-08-20
#>  3:     2021-07-10      1       DE       00+      64  2021-08-21
#>  4:     2021-07-11      1       DE       00+      48  2021-08-22
#>  5:     2021-07-12      1       DE       00+      17  2021-08-23
#>  6:     2021-07-13      1       DE       00+      59  2021-08-24
#>  7:     2021-07-14      1       DE       00+      72  2021-08-25
#>  8:     2021-07-15      1       DE       00+      69  2021-08-26
#>  9:     2021-07-16      1       DE       00+      47  2021-08-27
#> 10:     2021-07-17      1       DE       00+      65  2021-08-28
```

## Visualising the data

Before fitting any model, it is useful to plot the data to identify patterns that should inform model specification.


``` r
ggplot(latest_germany_hosp) +
  aes(x = reference_date, y = confirm) +
  geom_col(alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(
    x = "Reference date",
    y = "Hospitalisations"
  ) +
  theme_bw()
```

<div class="figure">
<img src="figures/getting-started-nowcasting-data-plot-1.png" alt="plot of chunk data-plot" width="100%" />
<p class="caption">plot of chunk data-plot</p>
</div>

The plot shows a clear day-of-week periodicity in hospitalisations alongside a strong upward trend.
These features will become important when we evaluate model performance below.

## Preprocessing

Before modelling, the input data needs to be converted into the "reporting triangle" format (see [our model description for details](https://package.epinowcast.org/articles/model)).
We also need to determine metadata to facilitate the model specification.
This includes the number of days of data to use for the reference and report modules, the maximum delay to consider, and, optionally, a grouping (i.e. age group, location, or both) of observations.


``` r
pobs <- enw_preprocess_data(
  retro_nat_germany, max_delay = 43
)
pobs
#> ── Preprocessed nowcast data ─────────────────────────────────────────────────── 
#> Groups: 1 | Timestep: day | Max delay: 43 
#> Observations: 43 timepoints x 43 snapshots 
#> Max date: 2021-08-19 
#> 
#> Datasets (access with `enw_get_data(x, "<name>")`): 
#>   obs                :     946 x 9 
#>   new_confirm        :     946 x 11 
#>   latest             :      43 x 10 
#>   missing_reference  :       0 x 6 
#>   reporting_triangle :      43 x 45 
#>   metareference      :      43 x 9 
#>   metareport         :      85 x 12 
#>   metadelay          :      43 x 5
```

The returned output is an `enw_preprocess_data` object.
Printing it shows the key metadata and dimensions of each nested dataset.
We can extract a specific dataset using `enw_get_data()`:


``` r
enw_get_data(pobs, "latest") |> head()
#>    reference_date report_date .group max_confirm location age_group confirm
#>            <IDat>      <IDat>  <num>       <int>   <fctr>    <fctr>   <int>
#> 1:     2021-07-08  2021-08-19      1          53       DE       00+      53
#> 2:     2021-07-09  2021-08-19      1          46       DE       00+      46
#> 3:     2021-07-10  2021-08-19      1          64       DE       00+      64
#> 4:     2021-07-11  2021-08-19      1          47       DE       00+      47
#> 5:     2021-07-12  2021-08-19      1          17       DE       00+      17
#> 6:     2021-07-13  2021-08-19      1          59       DE       00+      59
#>    cum_prop_reported delay prop_reported
#>                <num> <num>         <num>
#> 1:                 1    42             0
#> 2:                 1    41             0
#> 3:                 1    40             0
#> 4:                 1    39             0
#> 5:                 1    38             0
#> 6:                 1    37             0
```

For a more detailed overview including a preview of the reporting triangle, use `summary()`:


``` r
summary(pobs)
#> ── Preprocessed nowcast data summary ─────────────────────────────────────────── 
#> Groups: 1 | Timestep: day | Max delay: 43 
#> Date range: 2021-07-08 to 2021-08-19 (42 days) 
#> Observations: 43 timepoints x 43 snapshots 
#> 
#> Latest observations (first 6 rows): 
#>    reference_date report_date .group max_confirm location age_group confirm
#>            <IDat>      <IDat>  <num>       <int>   <fctr>    <fctr>   <int>
#> 1:     2021-07-08  2021-08-19      1          53       DE       00+      53
#> 2:     2021-07-09  2021-08-19      1          46       DE       00+      46
#> 3:     2021-07-10  2021-08-19      1          64       DE       00+      64
#> 4:     2021-07-11  2021-08-19      1          47       DE       00+      47
#> 5:     2021-07-12  2021-08-19      1          17       DE       00+      17
#> 6:     2021-07-13  2021-08-19      1          59       DE       00+      59
#>    cum_prop_reported delay prop_reported
#>                <num> <num>         <num>
#> 1:                 1    42             0
#> 2:                 1    41             0
#> 3:                 1    40             0
#> 4:                 1    39             0
#> 5:                 1    38             0
#> 6:                 1    37             0
#> 
#> Reporting triangle corner (first 6 rows x 8 cols): 
#> Key: <.group, reference_date>
#>    .group reference_date     0     1     2     3     4     5
#>     <num>         <IDat> <int> <int> <int> <int> <int> <int>
#> 1:      1     2021-07-08    25    13     2     1     0     2
#> 2:      1     2021-07-09    10    13     1     1     1     1
#> 3:      1     2021-07-10    23    11     2     5     7     1
#> 4:      1     2021-07-11    14     4     3     0     2     2
#> 5:      1     2021-07-12     3     3     2     0     0     2
#> 6:      1     2021-07-13    21    12     3     4     3     0
#> ... (43 rows x 45 cols total)
```

It can be useful to check this output before specifying the model, just to make sure everything is as expected.
The preprocessed object also supports a `plot()` method for exploring the delay structure visually; see the [visualising preprocessed data](preprocess-visualisation.html) vignette for details.

# The default model

The `epinowcast` package provides a modular modelling framework.
The package comes equipped with several modules that we can combine to construct models, and also allows us to create our own modules.
This ensures that models can be tailored to the specific data and context.

Each module defines its own default priors which can be inspected and customised.
See `?epinowcast` for details on inspecting and setting priors.

The default model uses a daily geometric random walk for the expected counts, a lognormal delay distribution, and no report-date effects.
This is a reasonable starting point for many settings but is not tuned for any particular problem.


``` r
model <- enw_model(threads = TRUE)
```


``` r
options(mc.cores = 2)
default_fit_opts <- enw_fit_opts(
  save_warmup = FALSE,
  pp = TRUE,
  chains = 2,
  threads_per_chain = 2,
  iter_sampling = 500,
  iter_warmup = 500,
  adapt_delta = 0.95,
  max_depth = 12,
  show_messages = interactive()
)
nowcast_default <- epinowcast(
  data = pobs,
  fit = default_fit_opts,
  model = model
)
```


``` r
nowcast_default
#> ── epinowcast model output ───────────────────────────────────────────────────── 
#> Groups: 1 | Timestep: day | Max delay: 43 
#> Observations: 43 timepoints x 43 snapshots 
#> Max date: 2021-08-19 
#> 
#> Datasets (access with `enw_get_data(x, "<name>")`): 
#>   obs                :     946 x 9 
#>   new_confirm        :     946 x 11 
#>   latest             :      43 x 10 
#>   missing_reference  :       0 x 6 
#>   reporting_triangle :      43 x 45 
#>   metareference      :      43 x 9 
#>   metareport         :      85 x 12 
#>   metadelay          :      43 x 5 
#> 
#> Priors: 14 parameters 
#>           variable          distribution  mean    sd
#>             <char>                <char> <num> <num>
#>         expr_r_int                Normal   0.0   0.2
#>       expr_beta_sd Zero truncated normal   0.0   1.0
#>  expr_lelatent_int                Normal   4.0   1.0
#>       expl_beta_sd Zero truncated normal   0.0   1.0
#>      refp_mean_int                Normal   1.0   1.0
#>        refp_sd_int Zero truncated normal   0.5   1.0
#>  refp_mean_beta_sd Zero truncated normal   0.0   1.0
#>    refp_sd_beta_sd Zero truncated normal   0.0   1.0
#>          refnp_int                Normal   0.0   1.0
#>      refnp_beta_sd Zero truncated normal   0.0   1.0
#>        rep_beta_sd Zero truncated normal   0.0   1.0
#>           miss_int                Normal   0.0   1.0
#>       miss_beta_sd Zero truncated normal   0.0   1.0
#>           sqrt_phi Zero truncated normal   0.0   0.5
#> Model fit: 
#> 
#>  Samples: 1,000 | Max Rhat: 1.01 
#> 
#>  Divergent transitions: 0 (0%) 
#>   Run time: 17.8 secs
```


``` r
plot(
  nowcast_default,
  latest_obs = latest_germany_hosp
)
```

<div class="figure">
<img src="figures/getting-started-nowcasting-nowcast-default-1.png" alt="plot of chunk nowcast-default" width="100%" />
<p class="caption">plot of chunk nowcast-default</p>
</div>

The default model captures the general level of hospitalisations but misses two features visible in the data.
First, the strong upward trend leads to underestimation of recent dates, where the most recent observations (triangles) fall above the credible intervals.
Second, the day-of-week periodicity visible in the data is not captured, as the default model has no weekly seasonal component.

# Improving the model

Based on these observations, we can build improved models.
We address the trend by replacing the daily geometric random walk with a weekly growth rate model, add day-of-week effects to both the expectation and report modules, and compare two different delay specifications.

## Process model

The underlying process model is an exponential growth rate model ($C_t = C_{t-1} \exp^{r_t}$).
To capture the sustained upward trend, we specify a weekly random walk on the growth rate with a baseline intercept.
We also add a day-of-week random effect to account for the weekly periodicity.


``` r
expectation_module <- enw_expectation(
  ~ 1 + rw(week) + (1 | day_of_week),
  data = pobs
)
```

The intercept provides a baseline growth rate, and `rw(week)` allows it to change each week without overfitting to daily noise.
The `(1 | day_of_week)` term captures the weekly seasonal pattern.

## Reference model: reporting delays

The default model assumes a lognormal delay distribution, which we keep for the first improved model.


``` r
lognormal_reference <- enw_reference(
  parametric = ~1,
  distribution = "lognormal",
  data = pobs
)
```

For the second improved model, we replace this with a non-parametric specification that makes no distributional assumption.
Instead, it estimates a separate reporting hazard for each delay interval using random effects, with partial pooling across delays.


``` r
np_reference <- enw_reference(
  parametric = ~0,
  non_parametric = ~0 + (1 | delay),
  data = pobs
)
```

## Reporting effects by report date

The default model had no report-date effects.
Here we add a day-of-week random effect and a weekly random walk to allow the reporting process to change over time.


``` r
report_module <- enw_report(
  ~ (1 | day_of_week) + rw(week), data = pobs
)
```

## Observation model

The default model uses a Poisson likelihood.
For the improved models, we switch to a negative binomial likelihood to allow for overdispersion.


``` r
obs_module <- enw_obs(family = "negbin", data = pobs)
```

## Fitting the improved models

We can now fit both improved models.
Since the Stan model is already compiled, only sampling time is required.


``` r
fit_opts <- enw_fit_opts(
  save_warmup = FALSE,
  pp = TRUE,
  chains = 2,
  threads_per_chain = 2,
  iter_sampling = 500,
  iter_warmup = 500,
  adapt_delta = 0.95,
  max_depth = 12,
  show_messages = interactive()
)
nowcast_lognormal <- epinowcast(
  data = pobs,
  expectation = expectation_module,
  reference = lognormal_reference,
  report = report_module,
  obs = obs_module,
  fit = fit_opts,
  model = model
)
```


``` r
nowcast_np <- epinowcast(
  data = pobs,
  expectation = expectation_module,
  reference = np_reference,
  report = report_module,
  obs = obs_module,
  fit = fit_opts,
  model = model
)
```

# Results

The `epinowcast()` function returns an `epinowcast` object which includes diagnostic information, the data used for fitting, and the underlying [`CmdStanModel` object](https://mc-stan.org/cmdstanr/reference/CmdStanModel.html).
Printing it shows key diagnostics including the number of samples, convergence (Rhat), and any divergent transitions.

## Diagnostics


``` r
nowcast_lognormal
#> ── epinowcast model output ───────────────────────────────────────────────────── 
#> Groups: 1 | Timestep: day | Max delay: 43 
#> Observations: 43 timepoints x 43 snapshots 
#> Max date: 2021-08-19 
#> 
#> Datasets (access with `enw_get_data(x, "<name>")`): 
#>   obs                :     946 x 9 
#>   new_confirm        :     946 x 11 
#>   latest             :      43 x 10 
#>   missing_reference  :       0 x 6 
#>   reporting_triangle :      43 x 45 
#>   metareference      :      43 x 9 
#>   metareport         :      85 x 12 
#>   metadelay          :      43 x 5 
#> 
#> Priors: 14 parameters 
#>           variable          distribution  mean    sd
#>             <char>                <char> <num> <num>
#>         expr_r_int                Normal   0.0   0.2
#>       expr_beta_sd Zero truncated normal   0.0   1.0
#>  expr_lelatent_int                Normal   4.0   1.0
#>       expl_beta_sd Zero truncated normal   0.0   1.0
#>      refp_mean_int                Normal   1.0   1.0
#>        refp_sd_int Zero truncated normal   0.5   1.0
#>  refp_mean_beta_sd Zero truncated normal   0.0   1.0
#>    refp_sd_beta_sd Zero truncated normal   0.0   1.0
#>          refnp_int                Normal   0.0   1.0
#>      refnp_beta_sd Zero truncated normal   0.0   1.0
#>        rep_beta_sd Zero truncated normal   0.0   1.0
#>           miss_int                Normal   0.0   1.0
#>       miss_beta_sd Zero truncated normal   0.0   1.0
#>           sqrt_phi Zero truncated normal   0.0   0.5
#> Model fit: 
#> 
#>  Samples: 1,000 | Max Rhat: 1.01 
#> 
#>  Divergent transitions: 0 (0%) 
#>   Run time: 128.6 secs
```


``` r
nowcast_np
#> ── epinowcast model output ───────────────────────────────────────────────────── 
#> Groups: 1 | Timestep: day | Max delay: 43 
#> Observations: 43 timepoints x 43 snapshots 
#> Max date: 2021-08-19 
#> 
#> Datasets (access with `enw_get_data(x, "<name>")`): 
#>   obs                :     946 x 9 
#>   new_confirm        :     946 x 11 
#>   latest             :      43 x 10 
#>   missing_reference  :       0 x 6 
#>   reporting_triangle :      43 x 45 
#>   metareference      :      43 x 9 
#>   metareport         :      85 x 12 
#>   metadelay          :      43 x 5 
#> 
#> Priors: 14 parameters 
#>           variable          distribution  mean    sd
#>             <char>                <char> <num> <num>
#>         expr_r_int                Normal   0.0   0.2
#>       expr_beta_sd Zero truncated normal   0.0   1.0
#>  expr_lelatent_int                Normal   4.0   1.0
#>       expl_beta_sd Zero truncated normal   0.0   1.0
#>      refp_mean_int                Normal   1.0   1.0
#>        refp_sd_int Zero truncated normal   0.5   1.0
#>  refp_mean_beta_sd Zero truncated normal   0.0   1.0
#>    refp_sd_beta_sd Zero truncated normal   0.0   1.0
#>          refnp_int                Normal   0.0   1.0
#>      refnp_beta_sd Zero truncated normal   0.0   1.0
#>        rep_beta_sd Zero truncated normal   0.0   1.0
#>           miss_int                Normal   0.0   1.0
#>       miss_beta_sd Zero truncated normal   0.0   1.0
#>           sqrt_phi Zero truncated normal   0.0   0.5
#> Model fit: 
#> 
#>  Samples: 1,000 | Max Rhat: 1.03 
#> 
#>  Divergent transitions: 0 (0%) 
#>   Run time: 241.1 secs
```

## Comparing all models

We can compare all three nowcasts by overlaying their posterior summaries.


``` r
default_summary <- summary(
  nowcast_default,
  probs = c(0.05, 0.2, 0.8, 0.95)
)
default_summary[, model := "Default"]

lognormal_summary <- summary(
  nowcast_lognormal,
  probs = c(0.05, 0.2, 0.8, 0.95)
)
lognormal_summary[, model := "Lognormal"]

np_summary <- summary(
  nowcast_np,
  probs = c(0.05, 0.2, 0.8, 0.95)
)
np_summary[, model := "Non-parametric"]

combined <- rbindlist(
  list(default_summary, lognormal_summary, np_summary),
  use.names = TRUE
)

ggplot(combined) +
  aes(x = reference_date) +
  geom_ribbon(
    aes(ymin = q5, ymax = q95, fill = model),
    alpha = 0.2
  ) +
  geom_ribbon(
    aes(ymin = q20, ymax = q80, fill = model),
    alpha = 0.3
  ) +
  geom_line(
    aes(y = median, colour = model),
    linewidth = 0.8
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = latest_germany_hosp,
    aes(y = confirm),
    shape = 2,
    size = 1.1,
    alpha = 0.7
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Reference date",
    y = "Hospitalisations",
    fill = "Model",
    colour = "Model"
  ) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

<div class="figure">
<img src="figures/getting-started-nowcasting-comparison-1.png" alt="plot of chunk comparison" width="100%" />
<p class="caption">plot of chunk comparison</p>
</div>

The default model captures the general level of hospitalisations but underestimates recent dates and misses the day-of-week pattern.
Both improved models better capture the upward trend and weekly periodicity.
The two improved models produce similar estimates for this dataset, where the delay distribution is approximately lognormal.
The non-parametric model shows slightly wider uncertainty for recent dates, reflecting the additional flexibility of estimating a separate hazard per delay interval.
In settings where the delay distribution is multi-modal or irregular, the non-parametric approach would show a larger advantage.

## Posterior predictions

Plotting posterior predictions can be a useful way of assessing whether the model captures the underlying data generation process adequately.


``` r
plot(nowcast_lognormal, type = "posterior") +
  facet_wrap(vars(reference_date), scale = "free")
```

<div class="figure">
<img src="figures/getting-started-nowcasting-pp-lognormal-1.png" alt="plot of chunk pp-lognormal" width="100%" />
<p class="caption">plot of chunk pp-lognormal</p>
</div>

## Using package functions rather than S3 methods

Rather than using S3 methods supplied for `epinowcast()` directly, package functions can also be used to extract nowcast posterior samples, summarise them, and then plot them.
This is demonstrated here by plotting the 7 day incidence for hospitalisations using the lognormal model.


``` r
# extract samples
samples <- summary(
  nowcast_lognormal, type = "nowcast_samples"
)

# Take a 7 day rolling sum of both samples and observations
cols <- c("confirm", "sample")
samples[, (cols) := lapply(.SD, frollsum, n = 7),
  .SDcols = cols, by = ".draw"
][!is.na(sample)]
#>        reference_date report_date .group max_confirm location age_group confirm
#>                <IDat>      <IDat>  <num>       <int>   <fctr>    <fctr>   <int>
#>     1:     2021-07-14  2021-08-19      1          72       DE       00+     358
#>     2:     2021-07-14  2021-08-19      1          72       DE       00+     358
#>     3:     2021-07-14  2021-08-19      1          72       DE       00+     358
#>     4:     2021-07-14  2021-08-19      1          72       DE       00+     358
#>     5:     2021-07-14  2021-08-19      1          72       DE       00+     358
#>    ---                                                                         
#> 36996:     2021-08-19  2021-08-19      1          84       DE       00+    1028
#> 36997:     2021-08-19  2021-08-19      1          84       DE       00+    1028
#> 36998:     2021-08-19  2021-08-19      1          84       DE       00+    1028
#> 36999:     2021-08-19  2021-08-19      1          84       DE       00+    1028
#> 37000:     2021-08-19  2021-08-19      1          84       DE       00+    1028
#>        cum_prop_reported delay prop_reported .chain .iteration .draw sample
#>                    <num> <num>         <num>  <int>      <int> <int>  <num>
#>     1:                 1    36             0      1          1     1    358
#>     2:                 1    36             0      1          2     2    358
#>     3:                 1    36             0      1          3     3    360
#>     4:                 1    36             0      1          4     4    358
#>     5:                 1    36             0      1          5     5    358
#>    ---                                                                     
#> 36996:                 1     0             1      2        496   996   1829
#> 36997:                 1     0             1      2        497   997   1802
#> 36998:                 1     0             1      2        498   998   1766
#> 36999:                 1     0             1      2        499   999   1760
#> 37000:                 1     0             1      2        500  1000   1838
latest_germany_hosp_7day <- copy(latest_germany_hosp)[
  ,
  confirm := frollsum(confirm, n = 7)
][!is.na(confirm)]

# Summarise samples
sum_across_last_7_days <- enw_summarise_samples(samples)

# Plot samples
enw_plot_nowcast_quantiles(
  sum_across_last_7_days, latest_germany_hosp_7day
)
```

<div class="figure">
<img src="figures/getting-started-nowcasting-week-nowcast-1.png" alt="plot of chunk week-nowcast" width="100%" />
<p class="caption">plot of chunk week-nowcast</p>
</div>

# Next steps

The progression from the default model to the improved specifications illustrates the iterative workflow that `epinowcast` is designed to support.
Each module can be independently adjusted based on what we observe in the data and results.

Further extensions to consider include:

- Combining parametric and non-parametric components in a single reference model to balance flexibility with parsimony.
- Allowing the delay distribution to vary over time if the reporting process changes.
- Stratifying by age group or location using hierarchical models.
- Formally comparing models using leave-one-out cross-validation or scoring rules.

See the [model description](https://package.epinowcast.org/articles/model) vignette for mathematical details, the [age-stratified nowcasting](https://package.epinowcast.org/articles/germany-age-stratified-nowcasting) vignette for hierarchical models and model comparison with LOO, and the [features](https://package.epinowcast.org/articles/features) vignette for a full overview of modelling options.
